posts.text: If you're a Head of TA and you haven't updated your hiring bar in the last 12 months, AI just made it obsolete. We built a free interactive guide to help you fix that. 1. The Shift- what crossed a threshold over winter 2025, and why the signals you've been hiring on no longer predict performance 2. By Function - Engineering, GTM, Design: old signals vs. new, side by side, with the specific markers that are losing predictive value in each 3. Skills Matrix - every competency, filterable by function, mapped from junior to senior, with a clear definition of what solid looks like vs. what exceptional looks like 4. The JD Rebuilder - see exactly what to cut, what to replace it with, and why. Copy-ready language for Engineering, GTM, and Design, ready to drop straight in 5. Interview Guide - 7 questions built to surface candidates who've adapted to AI, each one comes with what it signals, and the green and red flags. 6. Candidate Scorecard - a live scorecard. Rate candidates across five dimensions in real time and get an instant read on where they land Free. Interactive. 👉 Comment "AI GUIDE" and I'll send you the link.
posts.hook: If you're a Head of TA and you haven't updated your hiring bar in the last 12 months, AI just made it obsolete.
posts.name: Adriano Herdman

posts.text: We need to be an AI-native talent function! It comes up at the leadership meets. The Head of TA nods along and then is left holding the hot potato. And most of the time, it means nothing. Because whoever said it usually can’t follow it with anything concrete. “Figure out the AI thing before we open more roles” is where the sentence ends. No example. No idea what good actually looks like. That’s not a strategy. It’s a wish with a deadline attached. Heads of TA, here’s what’s getting blurred: Your recruiters can absolutely use AI to move faster. Sourcing, screening, writing up notes, all quicker. But speed isn’t reinvention. They’re optimising the job they already have. But. Rethinking how you hire altogether isn’t their call to make. It’s yours,  And you can’t rethink anything if you’ve never touched the tools yourself. You won’t think to ask: “Can we map every engineer at our 4 closest competitors, sort them by tenure and tech stack, and flag who’s likely to move this quarter?”, because you don’t know that’s now a 45-minute job instead of a 1-week project. The questions worth asking only surface once you’ve actually been in there, hands dirty, seeing what’s possible. A Head of TA who’s a beginner at this can’t lead it. You can’t be a thought partner to your team while waiting for them to hand you a deck you don’t know how to evaluate. And this runs all the way up. The CPO mandating it to their Head of TA. The CEO mandating it to the exec team. If you’re the one saying “we need to be AI-native” and you’ve never built and got your hands dirty, you’re passing down a slogan and calling it direction. The people getting ahead right now are doing it the other way round. They start with what the tools can actually do, then build the hiring strategy from there. Capability first, strategy second. If you want to lead hiring through this shift, get in the tools. Talking about them isn’t the same thing.
posts.hook: We need to be an AI-native talent function!
posts.name: Adriano Herdman

posts.text: How talent acquisition teams are using Claude in 2026. We ran conversations and research against Anthropic's guidance to map how they deploy Claude Result: 8 workflows, ranked by what shows up in the wild. Chat dominates. Screening, outreach, JDs, interview prep. The story is Claude Code and Cowork. Non-technical recruiters pointing Code at ATS exports. Cowork on resume folders. Tasks that took 4 hours now take 15 minutes. Then there's the edge: MCP servers into Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby. Live pipeline Q&A. Passive candidate nurturing loops. The productivity gap is widening. Any you'd add?
posts.hook: How talent acquisition teams are using Claude in 2026.
posts.name: Adriano Herdman

posts.text: Recruiters keep getting told AI is coming for their jobs. A product team at Anthropic accidentally convinced me of the exact opposite. Lenny Rachitsky shared his takeaways from a conversation with Catherine Wu, who runs Product for Claude Code, on how Anthropic actually builds. It was aimed at product people. But read it as a recruiter and the parallel hits you in the face. Their best engineers now go from user feedback to shipped product in a week. Solo. Because AI strips out the parts that used to need a whole team. The point was never speed. The point is that the smallest effective unit became one person with good judgement and the right tools. Here's where it gets interesting for us. That's the recruiter story too (if you let it be) One recruiter with AI in the loop can: - Write the spec - Build the intake brief - Prep the candidate pack - Draft the outreach …in the time it used to take to do just one of them. That's not a reason to shrink a team. It's leverage. The recruiters who pick this up aren't doing the same job faster. They're operating a level above the ones who don't. But the bigger unlock isn't time. It's quality. Every spec sharper. Every brief more thorough. Every message grounded in real outreach methodology instead of the same tired template. It's a sharp colleague reviewing your work before it goes out and pushing it higher. On every task. All day. Specs drafted straight off the hiring manager conversation. Candidate packs generated in minutes. Talent maps and TAM analysis that used to eat a full day. None of it replaced a recruiter. It raised the floor under all of them. And here's the part the engineering parallel can't reach: We work with people, not code. AI can draft the spec and the message. It cannot tell you who's right for a role, a manager and a team. The tools raise your ceiling. They don't touch the judgement that was always the actual job. So the real question for anyone running a TA function: Are you using AI to do the same work cheaper? Or to make your recruiters operate a level higher? Because those two go to very different places. Curious where you land on that one. 👇
posts.hook: Recruiters keep getting told AI is coming for their jobs.
posts.name: Adriano Herdman

posts.text: The average recruiter loses a full day every week to admin. None of it fills a role. Pipeline updates. Interview scheduling. Debrief notes. Offer letter drafts. Slack updates the hiring manager will forget to read. Time that should be going into actual hiring conversations. We've been building these workflows at Move and running them on live searches over the last few months. So we pulled together 14 Claude workflows we use ourselves, grouped by what recruiters actually do in a week: 1. Sourcing & targeting 2. Screening & decisions (shortlists, debrief summaries) 3. Outreach & marketing (candidate emails, inbound triage, LinkedIn assets) 4. Client & team reporting 5. Role ops (intake pack, scheduling, offer letters) Every row has the prompt to copy, the connectors needed, and a cadence. No agents to build. No code. You connect Drive, Notion, Gmail, Slack, Calendar, paste the prompt, type /schedule. You don't need to implement all 14 this week. Pick one. Get an hour back. Pick another next week. Raise the bar one workflow at a time. Everything here is human-in-the-loop. No yes/no decision gets made without the recruiter signing off. Claude facilitates the work and raises the floor on quality but the recruiter is the arbiter of every decision, and the arbiter of the inputs those decisions are made on. Built for those who'd rather spend their week talking to candidates than typing notes about them.
posts.hook: The average recruiter loses a full day every week to admin.
posts.name: Adriano Herdman

posts.text: 🧰 If you're a Head of TA running sourcing and relying on InMail, you're leaving 80% of replies on the table. This playbook fixes it. The best candidates for your hardest roles aren't applying. They're passive. Inbound won't reach them. And the stakes have never been higher. A senior engineer with AI tools today produces 5–10x what they produced in 2023. A bad senior hire now costs 5–10x more than two years ago. Who you hire, and whether you can reach them at all, compounds in ways it didn't before. Most teams are running outbound on InMail: one message, no follow-up, no feedback loop. That's not a sourcing strategy. It's a lottery. We've spent 5 years building and running sourcing campaigns across AI, engineering, GTM, and senior specialist roles. Hundreds of hires. We wrote down everything that works. It's called the Move Outbound Playbook. It's free. Inside: - How to run persona interviews before a single message is written - Message architecture that generates 40%+ reply rates - Why email sequences outperform InMail, with the cost data to prove it - How to map a talent market before you start sourcing, not after - How to turn rejection data into your most valuable hiring intelligence - The exact framework behind every campaign we run across the UK Europe, North America, and the Middle East. Comment "Outbound" below and I'll send it over.
posts.hook: 🧰 If you're a Head of TA running sourcing and relying on InMail, you're leaving 80% of replies on the table.
posts.name: Adriano Herdman

posts.text: Most TA teams don't measure one of the highest-leverage moments in the hiring process. The recruiter screen. AI has raised the floor on sourcing, outreach, scheduling, written comms. The recruiter screen is now the edge. We’ve pulled together the research from 4 disciplines that almost never talk to each other: 1. Sales discovery science   2. Structured interviewing research  3. Psychology of rapport   4. Candidate experience 11 slides. Scroll through ↓ Tag a Talent pro who might be interested.
